content.full_text:
Sacramento Address
State Capitol
P.O. Box 942849
Sacramento, CA 94249-0001
Telephone (916) 445-8800
Fax (916) 324-6882

District Office Address
3683 Crenshaw Boulevard
5th Floor
Los Angeles, California 90016
Telephone (213) 292-0605
Fax (213) 736-3209

Assembly
California Legislature

COMMITTEES:
Consumer Protection,
Governmental Efficiency,
and Economic Development
Education
Governmental Organization
Insurance
Televising the Assembly
Utilities and Commerce
Chairwoman
Select Committee on the
Office of the State Board of
Equalization

GWEN MOORE
ASSEMBLYWOMAN, FORTY-NINTH DISTRICT

MAJORITY WHIP

[stamp: AUG 3 1992]

July 31, 1992

John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J. Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Wallace,

     This letter is to follow-up on our telephone conversation
earlier today regarding honoraria, conflict-of-interest, and
legitimate business activities.

     As you will recall, I was asking you whether legislative
employees can do any writing for pay. I have worked as a
journalist in the past (I was the managing editor of the Furniture
Workers Press, and have done free-lance articles for pay), and I
am interested in supplementing my income now by doing free-lance
writing for pay.

     I have enclosed an article I just wrote on discovering my
family roots. Since this article would likely have little or no
relation to any bill that I would work on, would it be permissible
for me to sell it to a newspaper or magazine?

     If so, would it be permissible for a legislative employee to
sell an article on a political topic, as long as that employee
would not work on any legislation related to that article?

     Is it permissible for a legislator or legislative employee to
engage in a private profit-making business, say manufacturing
brooms, while drawing a pay-check from the legislature? If that is
permissible, why would it not be permissible for a legislator or
legislative employee to earn money from a writing business? If
the legislative employee/manufacturer is allowed to earn money in
his or her manufacturing business, wouldn't it be discriminatory
to prohibit the legislative employee/writer from earning money in
his or her writing business? Would the legislative employee/broom
manufacturer, who employees workers, be allowed to work on
legislation that impacted broom manufacturers, or people who use
brooms in the cleaning business, or people who work for a wage?

Printed on Recycled Paper

John Wallace-2


Might it be permissable for a legislative employee to engage
in another business, including writing, if he or she engaged in this
other business solely as a business-person, and not as a
legislator or legislative employee and was not identified as such?
Or could a distinction be made between a legislator and
legislative employee? Could a distinction be made between a
newspaper or other media which solicited a contribution for pay
from a legislator or legislative employee, and a legislative
employee who has a free-lance writing business, who took the
initiative to sell his or her writing, just as any free-lance
writer would?

What if the writing was on a topic not related to
legislation?

Further, with a freeze on legislative salaries, and recent
increases in legislative employee co-payments for health benefits,
is it fair to prohibit legislative employees from supplementing
their shrinking income by working as free-lance writers?

If it is permissable for a bonafide writer, who currently
works for the legislature, to sell his or her writing for pay,
what does that writer have to do to establish himself or herself
as a bonafide writer?

Thank you for your assistance in this matter.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]
Paul Krehbiel
Field Deputy
Assemblywoman Gwen Moore

embedding.qa_text: **Assembly California Legislature** GWEN MOORE ASSEMBLYWOMAN, FORTY-NINTH DISTRICT MAJORITY WHIP July 31, 1992 John Wallace Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J. Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95804 Dear Mr. Wallace, This letter is to follow-up on our telephone conversation earlier today regarding honoraria, conflict-of-interest, and legitimate business activities. As you will recall, I was asking you whether legislative employees can do any writing for pay. I have worked as a journalist in the past (I was the managing editor of the Furniture Workers Press, and have done free-lance articles for pay), and I am interested in supplementing my income now by doing free-lance writing for pay. I have enclosed an article I just wrote on discovering my family roots. Since this article would likely have little or no relation to any bill that I would work on, would it be permissible for me to sell it to a newspaper or magazine? If so, would it be permissable for a legislative employee to sell an article on a political topic, as long as that employee would not work on any legislation related to that article? Is it permissable for a legislator or legislative employee to engage in a private, profit-making business, say manufacturing brooms, while drawing a paycheck from the legislature? If that is permissable, why would it not be permissable for a legislator or legislative employee to earn money from a writing business? If the legislative employee/manufacturer is allowed to earn money in his or her manufacturing business, wouldn’t it be discriminatory to prohibit the legislative employee/writer from earning money in his or her writing business? Would the legislative employee/broom manufacturer, who employees workers, be allowed to work on legislation that impacted broom manufacturers, or people who use brooms in the cleaning business, or people who work for a wage? John Wallace-2 Might it be permissable for a legislative employee to engage in another business, including writing, if he or she acted in this other business solely as a business-person, and not as a legislator or legislative employee and was not identified as such? Or could a distinction be made between a legislator and legislative employee? Could a distinction be made between a newspaper or other media which solicited a contribution for pay from a legislator or legislative employee, and a legislative employee who has a free-lance writing business, who took the initiative to sell his or her writing, just as any free-lance writer would? What if the writing was on a topic not related to legislation? Further, with a freeze on legislative salaries, and recent increases in legislative employee co-payments for health benefits, is it fair to prohibit legislative employees from supplementing their shrinking income by working as free-lance writers? If it is permissable for a bonafide writer, who currently works for the legislature, to sell his or her writing for pay, what does that writer have to do to establish himself or herself as a bonafide writer? Thank you for your assistance in this matter. Sincerely yours, Paul
